relevant_files.role: Core security scanning logic using regex-based PatternEngine
PatternEngine that compiles and executes regex patterns for vulnerability detection
Embedded JSON file containing 14+ regex patterns for security scanning
Data structures for security findings, severity, and confidence levels
Performance benchmarks validating regex-based scanning completes in <10ms
Integration tests for security scanning functionality
Test fixtures with hardcoded secrets and SQL injection patterns
Test fixtures with safe code patterns
Documentation describing current security scanning capabilities and SARIF integration

conventions.commits: Conventional Commits with signed-off-by (DCO) required. Types: feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, perf, test, chore. Breaking changes marked with ! or BREAKING CHANGE footer.
conventions.testing: Unit tests in same file as code, integration tests in crates/aptu-core/tests/. Test files use assert! macros and standard Rust testing. CI runs cargo test, cargo clippy -- -D warnings, cargo fmt --check.
conventions.linting: clippy with deny-level warnings enforced in CI. rustfmt for code formatting. Additional linters: cargo deny for security advisories.
conventions.error_handling: Uses thiserror and anyhow for error handling. Tracing for logging. Follows Rust idioms for Result propagation.

patterns: Regex-based pattern matching operating line-by-line (known limitation per #735)
Hybrid approach: PatternEngine compiles regex patterns from JSON, SecurityScanner orchestrates scanning of diffs and files
SARIF output generation for GitHub Code Scanning integration

related_issues.title: feat(security): evaluate tree-sitter for AST-based vulnerability detection
related_issues.relevance: Primary issue requesting evaluation of tree-sitter as alternative to regex-based scanning

related_issues.title: test(security): add negative test documenting multi-line detection limitation
related_issues.relevance: Documents the known limitation that regex scanner cannot detect vulnerabilities where source and sink are on different lines

related_issues.title: PR that added the test from issue #735
related_issues.relevance: Implements the test case documenting the multi-line limitation

constraints: Current regex-based scanner operates line-by-line, preventing detection of multi-line vulnerabilities (source on one line, sink on another)
Adding tree-sitter requires language-specific grammar crates (tree-sitter-rust, tree-sitter-python, etc.) which increase binary size
Tree-sitter queries use Scheme-like syntax requiring learning curve
Data-flow/taint tracking still requires custom logic beyond AST parsing
Must maintain backward compatibility with existing regex patterns and SecurityScanner API
test_coverage: Comprehensive test coverage for regex-based scanner including: unit tests for scanner creation and scanning logic, integration tests for security scanning, negative test documenting multi-line limitation, benchmarks validating <10ms scan time for 500-line files. Test fixtures cover hardcoded secrets, SQL injection, path traversal, weak crypto, XSS, and safe code patterns. However, no tests exist for multi-line vulnerability detection (known limitation).

relevant_api: Parser::parse() - parse source code into TSTree
tree_sitter::Node - navigate syntax tree with methods like child(), child_by_field_name(), start_position(), end_position()
tree_sitter::Query - execute S-expression queries against syntax tree
Query::captures() - get captured nodes from queries
library_findings.notes: Tree-sitter Rust API provides efficient parsing and incremental updates. Language grammars available for Rust, Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, Java, PHP, and many others. Query syntax enables precise pattern matching based on syntax nodes rather than text patterns.

relevant_api: LANGUAGE constant - the Rust language grammar
library_findings.notes: Provides syntax tree for Rust code with node types like function_item, call_expression, binary_expression, identifier, etc. Enables detection of multi-line vulnerabilities by tracking data flow through syntax nodes.

approaches.name: Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter Scanner
approaches.description: Maintain existing regex-based scanner for simple patterns while adding tree-sitter-based scanner for multi-line and language-aware detection. Use tree-sitter to parse files and detect patterns where source and sink are on different lines, while keeping regex for single-line patterns like hardcoded secrets.
pros: Minimal disruption to existing codebase and API
Maintains backward compatibility with existing regex patterns
Gradual migration path with clear benefits
Language-specific parsing enables more accurate detection
Incremental parsing supports fast re-scanning on edits
cons: Increased binary size from grammar crates (5-10MB per language)
Learning curve for tree-sitter queries (Scheme-like syntax)

approaches.name: Full Migration to Tree-Sitter
approaches.description: Replace regex-based PatternEngine with tree-sitter-based scanning entirely. Convert all 14+ regex patterns to tree-sitter queries. Implement data-flow analysis for taint tracking across syntax nodes.
cons: High complexity - requires rewriting all patterns
Large increase in binary size (all language grammars)
Requires comprehensive testing of new approach

approaches.name: Tree-Sitter as Optional Enhancement Layer
approaches.description: Add tree-sitter as an optional enhancement that runs after regex scanning. Only activated when regex finds potential issues or when explicitly requested. Maintains existing API and behavior by default.
pros: Users can opt-in to enhanced scanning
Easy to disable if issues arise
Can be enabled per-file or per-project
cons: Misses multi-line vulnerabilities unless regex finds something to trigger tree-sitter
Two scanning passes may impact performance
Complexity in coordinating between two scanners
Not a complete solution to the core limitation

recommendation: Hybrid Regex + Tree-Sitter Scanner - This approach provides the best balance between solving the multi-line detection limitation while minimizing disruption to the existing codebase. It maintains backward compatibility, allows gradual adoption, and leverages the strengths of both regex (simple patterns) and tree-sitter (multi-line, language-aware patterns). The complexity is manageable (medium) and the benefits are significant (enabling detection of vulnerabilities that are currently missed). Start with a spike implementation detecting SQL injection across multiple lines in Rust code, then expand to other languages and patterns.
